refined_fr.refined_fr: Enable measurement and reporting of material throughput in volumetric or mass units per unit time.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) the system's capability to measure/report throughput metrics, and (2) the achievement of a customer-defined target. The latter is a success criterion dependent on operational execution, not a system requirement. The refined FR isolates the system's functional obligation—providing visibility into throughput—which is solution-neutral and testable independent of whether a specific productivity target is met. The target itself belongs in acceptance criteria or KPI definitions, not in the FR.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Demonstrate that the NGHE solution captures and reports material throughput (m³/h or ton/h) during representative operations. Verify data accuracy against independent measurement. Confirm reporting is accessible to Site Manager and Equipment Operator in real time or near-real time.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-001.1: Acquire material throughput data (volume or mass per time interval) during active operations.
FR-NGHE-001.2: Calculate and aggregate throughput metrics from acquired data.
FR-NGHE-001.3: Display or export throughput metrics to authorized stakeholders (Site Manager, Equipment Operator) in a human-readable format.

candidate_dps: DP1: Acquire instantaneous flow rate via continuous sensing of material stream properties (mass or volume per unit time) and integrate over evaluation period to compute cumulative throughput
DP2: Measure discrete material batches (count, mass, or volume per batch) at regular intervals and extrapolate to throughput rate over evaluation period
DP3: Monitor material level change in a bounded reservoir (input hopper or output stockpile) over time and infer net throughput from level differential and material density
DP4: Combine multiple independent measurement modalities (e.g., batch counting + continuous sensing) with cross-validation logic to report throughput with confidence bounds
DP5: Establish a calibrated reference baseline (e.g., manual tally or certified scale) and periodically reconcile reported throughput against reference to detect drift and adjust reporting
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP1: Acquire instantaneous flow rate via continuous sensing of material stream properties (mass or volume per unit time) and integrate over evaluation period to compute cumulative throughput

potential_sources: Coupling between sensor placement (IM choice) and flow uniformity assumption—if material distribution is non-uniform, single-point sensing may not represent true throughput; mitigation: specify multi-point sensing or homogenization requirement in DP refinement

Coupling between integration method (DP logic) and sensor noise/drift—low-quality instantaneous readings compound over long evaluation periods; mitigation: require sensor calibration and drift-correction procedures as part of DP realization

Coupling between material property variability (density, moisture) and mass-to-volume conversion—if density is not constant, mass and volume throughput may diverge; mitigation: specify which unit (mass or volume) is primary and document assumptions about material homogeneity

Coupling between reporting frequency and acceptance criteria evaluation—if throughput is reported at coarse intervals, transient underperformance may be masked; mitigation: align reporting cadence with evaluation period granularity
coupling_check.notes: DP1 is logically sound and decoupled from specific sensor technology. Couplings identified are between DP and IM (sensor placement, calibration procedures) and between DP assumptions (material uniformity, density stability) and operational context. These are manageable via explicit IM design and operational constraints, not fundamental DP flaws.

assumptions_risks: Material flow is continuous and approximately steady-state during evaluation period; risk: transient startup/shutdown phases or batch-mode operations may violate assumption—mitigation: define evaluation window to exclude transients or refine DP to handle batch boundaries

Instantaneous flow rate can be measured with sufficient accuracy and precision to integrate reliably; risk: sensor noise, calibration drift, or hysteresis may accumulate error—mitigation: specify sensor accuracy class (e.g., ±2% of reading) and require periodic recalibration

Material properties (density, particle size distribution) remain stable during evaluation period; risk: moisture absorption, segregation, or material substitution may alter effective throughput—mitigation: establish material acceptance criteria and environmental controls

Reported throughput is compared against productivity_target using the same units and measurement methodology; risk: ambiguity in target definition (e.g., is it net or gross throughput, accounting for spillage/recycle?) may invalidate acceptance decision—mitigation: explicitly define productivity_target in same units as reported throughput and document any exclusions

Evaluation period is long enough to average out short-term fluctuations and represent true operational capability; risk: if period is too short, random variation may cause false pass/fail—mitigation: specify minimum evaluation duration (e.g., 8 hours, 1 shift, 1 week) based on process variability
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine FR to explicitly state: (1) whether throughput is measured as mass per unit time or volume per unit time (or both), (2) whether the evaluation period includes or excludes startup/shutdown transients, (3) the definition of productivity_target (net or gross, accounting for losses), and (4) the required measurement accuracy (e.g., ±5% of target). Refine DP1 to specify: (a) the sensing principle (e.g., load cell, Coriolis meter, radar level gauge) shall be selected to achieve stated accuracy under worksite environmental conditions, (b) integration method (e.g., trapezoidal rule, running average) and sampling frequency, (c) calibration and drift-correction procedures, and (d) handling of measurement gaps or sensor faults. Introduce DP5 (periodic reconciliation against reference) as a complementary DP to detect systematic bias and build confidence in reported throughput.

im_boundary.name: Vehicle
im_boundary.scope: Physical and logical boundary encompassing all onboard sensing, actuation, and real-time data acquisition systems including flow rate measurement hardware, sensor integration, signal conditioning, and cumulative throughput computation

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Vehicle transmits instantaneous flow rate and cumulative throughput measurements to external systems (NGHE Solution, Teleoperation) via telemetry channel

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Vehicle receives evaluation period parameters, sensor calibration commands, and flow measurement mode directives from external tasking authority

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Vehicle uses network link for bidirectional communication of sensor data and control signals if remote sensing or distributed computation is required

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent containment: continuous sensing hardware, signal acquisition, integration logic, and cumulative computation are all vehicle-resident subsystems. Minimal diffusion: only telemetry output and tasking input cross boundary; core DP logic remains encapsulated.

Poor containment: NGHE Solution is a high-level orchestration/planning layer, not a sensing platform. Flow rate acquisition hardware and real-time sensor integration would have to diffuse into Vehicle anyway. Computation could reside here but would require continuous data streaming from Vehicle, creating tight coupling and latency sensitivity.

Very poor containment: Teleoperation is a human-machine interface and command relay layer. Sensing hardware and real-time integration must remain on Vehicle. Placing computation logic in Teleoperation creates unacceptable latency and dependency on network availability for safety-critical flow measurement.

Poor containment: Safety Services is a monitoring and alert layer, not a primary sensing/measurement subsystem. Flow rate acquisition and integration are operational measurement concerns, not safety-specific. Placing DP here would fragment measurement logic and create unnecessary coupling to safety infrastructure.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Vehicle module is the natural and optimal boundary for DP1 because it is the physical platform that carries the material stream, hosts the sensing hardware, and operates in real-time. Continuous sensing of flow properties (mass or volume per unit time) and integration over an evaluation period are inherently vehicle-resident operations. The DP is fully contained within Vehicle's scope; external systems consume the computed throughput via Telemetry and Tasking interfaces without needing to know implementation details.
containment_risks: Risk: If flow rate sensing requires external reference standards or calibration data from NGHE Solution, a dependency loop may emerge. Mitigation: Calibration parameters should be passed via Tasking interface at initialization; real-time sensing remains Vehicle-resident.

Risk: If evaluation period or integration window is dynamically commanded by Teleoperation or NGHE Solution, Vehicle must respond to Tasking updates. Mitigation: Tasking interface is already defined; Vehicle treats period updates as configuration changes without diffusing core measurement logic.

Risk: If Safety Services requires real-time flow rate for anomaly detection, it may request high-frequency telemetry. Mitigation: Vehicle publishes Telemetry at appropriate cadence; Safety Services consumes published data without owning measurement logic.

Risk: Network latency or packet loss could corrupt cumulative throughput if computation is distributed. Mitigation: Keep integration logic on Vehicle; transmit only final or periodic summaries via Telemetry to avoid synchronization issues.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. The FR acceptance criterion (reported productivity ≥ productivity_target over agreed evaluation period) is directly measurable via the Vehicle's Telemetry interface. Cumulative throughput computation is deterministic and auditable. Evaluation period is a tasking parameter, making acceptance testable under controlled conditions.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. The Vehicle module owns the complete measurement chain: sensing hardware, signal conditioning, integration logic, and cumulative computation. External systems (NGHE Solution, Teleoperation, Safety Services) consume results via Telemetry without owning measurement responsibility. Tasking interface provides configuration authority without fragmenting DP logic.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP1 (continuous sensing + integration over evaluation period) directly and sufficiently addresses FR (enable measurement and reporting of throughput in volumetric or mass units per unit time). The DP is solution-complete: it specifies the mechanism (instantaneous flow rate acquisition via continuous sensing) and the aggregation method (integration over period). No logical gaps or ambiguities detected. Acceptance criteria are traceable to DP outputs (cumulative throughput value).

diffusion_risks: Calibration dependency: If flow rate sensing requires external reference standards or calibration coefficients from NGHE Solution, a soft dependency emerges. Mitigation is adequate (calibration via Tasking at initialization), but monitor for runtime recalibration requests that could force Vehicle to poll external systems during measurement windows.

Evaluation period dynamism: If Teleoperation or NGHE Solution frequently updates evaluation period parameters mid-operation, Vehicle must handle Tasking updates without losing integration state. Current design assumes period is set at initialization; confirm that Tasking interface supports safe period reconfiguration (e.g., flush current accumulator, restart integration on new period).

Telemetry cadence coupling: If Safety Services or external monitoring systems demand high-frequency telemetry (e.g., every 100 ms) for anomaly detection, Vehicle's communication bandwidth and power budget may be stressed. Mitigation (Vehicle publishes at appropriate cadence; consumers adapt) is sound, but define 'appropriate cadence' explicitly in Telemetry interface specification to avoid implicit coupling.

Network-distributed computation temptation: The IM boundary correctly rejects distributed integration (e.g., Vehicle sends raw samples to NGHE Solution for aggregation). However, if future requirements demand edge analytics or cloud-based post-processing, ensure that only final/periodic summaries are transmitted, not raw sample streams, to avoid synchronization and latency issues.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Excellent containment (0.92 score) is justified. The Vehicle module is the natural owner: it hosts sensing hardware, operates in real-time, and maintains integration state without external dependency. Core DP logic (continuous sensing + integration) is fully encapsulated. Boundary crossing is minimal and well-defined: Telemetry (output) and Tasking (input) are clean, unidirectional interfaces. No evidence of DP diffusion across Vehicle, NGHE Solution, Teleoperation, or Safety Services. Alternative module candidates (NGHE, Teleoperation, Safety Services) all show poor containment (0.3–0.55) because they lack sensing hardware, real-time capability, or measurement ownership. Recommendation: Vehicle is the correct and only viable IM boundary for DP1.

recommended_revisions: Explicitly define Tasking interface contract for evaluation period updates: specify whether period can be changed mid-integration, whether Vehicle flushes or carries over accumulator state, and what acknowledgment/confirmation Vehicle returns to tasking authority.

Specify Telemetry cadence and data format in interface specification: define minimum/maximum publish frequency, latency tolerance, and whether cumulative throughput is transmitted as absolute value, delta, or both. This prevents implicit coupling to Safety Services or external monitoring systems.

Document calibration parameter lifecycle: clarify whether calibration coefficients are static (set once at initialization via Tasking) or dynamic (updated periodically). If dynamic, specify update mechanism and whether Vehicle pauses/resumes integration during calibration.

Add a note to IM boundary scope clarifying that 'real-time data acquisition systems' includes local buffering and state management for integration logic, ensuring that network latency or packet loss does not corrupt cumulative throughput computation.

Consider adding a diagnostic/health telemetry output (e.g., sensor status, integration window progress, calibration validity) to support troubleshooting without requiring external systems to infer measurement reliability from throughput values alone.

decision.justification: Gate results confirm FR-DP-IM alignment with high verification feasibility and excellent containment (0.92). However, safety criticality (high) and identified diffusion risks (calibration dependency, evaluation period dynamism, telemetry cadence coupling, distributed computation temptation) warrant decomposition of DP1 into sub-level FRs to expose and manage these risks explicitly. The current level is logically sound but architecturally incomplete: it does not yet specify how to ensure measurement integrity under failure modes, how to handle calibration lifecycle, or how to guarantee evaluation period consistency. Decomposition will force these concerns into the design record and enable allocation of safety responsibilities across sub-modules (sensing, integration, telemetry, tasking). Recommended revisions are implementation-level details that should be driven by sub-FR analysis, not applied retroactively.
next_level_sub_frs: FR1.1: Acquire instantaneous flow rate (or mass rate) from sensing hardware with specified accuracy and temporal resolution.
FR1.2: Integrate instantaneous flow rate over a defined evaluation period to compute cumulative throughput.
FR1.3: Manage evaluation period lifecycle (initialization, reconfiguration, state consistency) without losing or corrupting integration state.
FR1.4: Maintain calibration parameters and apply them consistently to flow rate acquisition throughout the measurement window.

FR1.5: Publish cumulative throughput and measurement metadata (period, calibration status, sensor health) via Telemetry at an appropriate cadence without introducing external dependencies or network-induced latency.
FR1.6: Detect and report measurement anomalies or integrity violations (e.g., sensor failure, integration overflow, calibration drift) to enable safety-critical decision-making.

revise.why: DP1 is logically correct but must be refined to explicitly address sub-FR concerns. Current DP ('continuous sensing + integration over evaluation period') is a black box that obscures: (a) sensor acquisition strategy (sampling rate, filtering, error handling), (b) integration algorithm robustness (accumulator overflow, state recovery), (c) calibration application (when, how, validation), (d) evaluation period state machine (initialization, update, flush logic), and (e) telemetry publishing strategy (cadence, format, metadata). Decomposition will force DP refinement at each sub-level, ensuring that safety-critical concerns (measurement integrity, failure detection, state consistency) are explicitly designed and testable. Recommend deferring detailed DP revisions until sub-FR analysis is complete, then roll up refined DPs into a coherent DP1 specification.

assumptions: Evaluation period is set at initialization and remains stable during a measurement window; if mid-window updates are required, Vehicle can safely flush accumulator and restart integration without losing critical data.

Flow rate sensing hardware provides continuous or near-continuous output (e.g., analog signal, digital stream) with known accuracy and temporal resolution; no gaps or dropouts are expected during normal operation.
Calibration parameters are static or updated infrequently (e.g., at initialization or during scheduled maintenance); runtime recalibration does not occur during active measurement windows.
Telemetry interface can support periodic publication of cumulative throughput without imposing real-time constraints that would force Vehicle to synchronize with external systems.
Safety Services and external monitoring systems do not require raw sensor samples; they consume only final throughput values and metadata, enabling Vehicle to aggregate and filter locally.
Network latency and packet loss are acceptable within the Telemetry interface design; Vehicle does not rely on acknowledgment from consumers to validate measurement integrity.
open_risks: Calibration dependency: If flow rate sensing requires external reference standards or dynamic recalibration during operation, Vehicle may need to poll NGHE Solution or external systems, introducing soft coupling and potential latency. Mitigation: Decompose FR1.4 to specify calibration lifecycle and validate that static or infrequent updates are sufficient.

Evaluation period dynamism: If Teleoperation or NGHE Solution updates evaluation period mid-operation, Vehicle must handle state consistency (flush vs. carry-over). Current design assumes static period; confirm via FR1.3 decomposition that safe reconfiguration is feasible.

Telemetry cadence coupling: If Safety Services demand high-frequency telemetry (e.g., every 100 ms), Vehicle's communication and power budget may be stressed. Mitigation: Decompose FR1.5 to define 'appropriate cadence' explicitly and validate that consumers can adapt to Vehicle's publishing rate.

Measurement integrity under failure: No explicit DP for detecting sensor failure, integration overflow, or calibration drift. High safety criticality demands that FR1.6 be decomposed to specify failure detection and reporting mechanisms.

State recovery and resilience: If Vehicle loses power or network connectivity during measurement, integration state may be lost. Decompose FR1.2 and FR1.3 to specify whether state is persisted, how recovery is handled, and whether measurement window is extended or restarted.

Distributed computation temptation: Future requirements may push for edge analytics or cloud-based post-processing. Ensure IM boundary remains firm: only final summaries are transmitted, not raw samples.
evidence_needed: Specification of flow rate sensing hardware: accuracy, temporal resolution, output format, failure modes, and calibration requirements.
Definition of evaluation period: typical duration, range of valid values, and whether mid-window updates are operationally required.
Telemetry interface specification: publish frequency (minimum/maximum), latency tolerance, data format (absolute throughput, delta, metadata), and consumer expectations.
Tasking interface specification: contract for evaluation period updates, calibration parameter injection, and Vehicle acknowledgment/confirmation.
Safety requirements allocation: which measurement concerns (sensor failure, integration overflow, calibration drift) are safety-critical and require explicit detection/reporting.
Network and power budget analysis: confirm that Telemetry cadence and payload size are feasible given Vehicle's communication and energy constraints.
Calibration lifecycle documentation: whether calibration is static, periodic, or dynamic; how coefficients are obtained and validated; and whether Vehicle pauses/resumes integration during updates.
State persistence and recovery strategy: whether integration state is logged, how recovery is handled after power loss or network outage, and whether measurement window is extended or restarted.
